questions.question: Select three elements of high-quality CPR.
choices: Chest compressions of adequate depth and rate
Full chest recoil
Minimize interruptions
Intentionally perform hyperventilation
Carefully check for a pulse for more than 30 seconds each time
questions.explanation: High-quality CPR requires adequate compressions, full recoil, and minimizing interruptions. Hyperventilation and prolonged interruptions should be avoided.

questions.question: Select all appropriate priorities in managing cardiac arrest due to VF/pulseless VT.
choices: Prioritize epinephrine administration over defibrillation
Resume CPR immediately after defibrillation
Use epinephrine and antiarrhythmic drugs if defibrillation is unsuccessful
Perform defibrillation while continuing chest compressions
Assess ECPR indications early
questions.explanation: In VF/pulseless VT, defibrillation is the top priority. CPR should be resumed immediately after defibrillation, and epinephrine and antiarrhythmic drugs used as needed. ECPR indications should be considered early.

questions.question: Select three reversible causes included in the 5H5T framework for PEA/asystole.
choices: Hypoxia
Hypovolemia
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Hyperkalemia
Natural aging
questions.explanation: The reversible causes of PEA/asystole are the 5Hs (Hypoxia, Hypovolemia, Hypothermia, Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Acidosis) and 5Ts (Tamponade, Tension pneumothorax, Toxins, Thrombosis-pulmonary, Thrombosis-coronary).

questions.question: Select three correct actions when using an AED.
choices: Place electrode pads in the correct positions
Confirm that no one is touching the patient during rhythm analysis and shock delivery
Resume chest compressions immediately after the shock
Stop all chest compressions while the AED is attached
Apply pads to a wet chest without drying it
questions.explanation: Correct pad placement, safety checks, and immediate CPR resumption after the shock are key when using an AED. Interruptions other than during analysis and shock delivery should be minimized.

questions.question: An unresponsive adult patient is found on the ward. Select two correct initial assessments for a healthcare provider.
choices: Check breathing and carotid pulse within 10 seconds
If agonal breathing is present, treat as breathing present and do not start CPR
If uncertain whether breathing is normal, start CPR
Check pulse at the radial artery
Always perform head-tilt chin-lift before checking breathing
questions.explanation: Breathing and carotid pulse should be assessed within 10 seconds. Start CPR if in doubt or if agonal breathing is present.

questions.question: Regarding chest compressions in adult cardiac arrest, select the ONE incorrect statement.
choices: Compress the lower half of the sternum
Target a compression depth of approximately 5 cm, not exceeding 6 cm
Target a compression rate of 100–120 per minute
Full chest recoil is unnecessary
Continue chest compressions until just before ECG analysis/rhythm check
questions.explanation: Full chest recoil during chest compressions is essential and must not be omitted.

questions.question: Regarding management of adult VF/pulseless VT, select all correct statements.
choices: Resume CPR starting with chest compressions immediately after the shock
Check carotid pulse first after the shock
As a general rule, perform rhythm checks every 2 minutes thereafter
Use a manual defibrillator to deliver a shock for VF/pulseless VT
Withhold chest compressions until a QRS complex appears after the shock
questions.explanation: For VF/pulseless VT, resume CPR immediately after the shock and re-evaluate the rhythm approximately every 2 minutes.

questions.question: Regarding vascular access for resuscitation drug administration in adult cardiac arrest, select the ONE correct statement.
choices: Central venous access is faster than peripheral IV and is the first choice
Intraosseous access is for pediatric use only even when peripheral IV is difficult
When establishing new drug delivery access, peripheral IV is the first choice
Intraosseous access can only be used after ROSC
Endotracheal drug administration is still the standard first choice in adult ALS
questions.explanation: Peripheral IV access is the first choice for resuscitation drug delivery; intraosseous access is used when peripheral IV is difficult.

questions.question: Regarding epinephrine in adult cardiac arrest, select the ONE incorrect statement.
choices: The usual dose is 1 mg IV per administration
Repeat dosing every 3–5 minutes
For non-shockable rhythms, administer as early as possible when given
Improvements in ROSC rate and survival to hospital discharge have been demonstrated
It is strongly recommended because it clearly improves favorable neurological outcomes
questions.explanation: Epinephrine improves ROSC rate and survival to hospital discharge, but evidence for improvement in neurological outcomes is limited.

questions.question: Regarding pharmacotherapy for refractory VF/pulseless VT, select three correct statements.
choices: Consider amiodarone 300 mg IV
Antiarrhythmic drugs reliably improve favorable neurological outcomes
Nifekalant may be used if amiodarone is unavailable
Lidocaine may be used if amiodarone is unavailable
The initial dose of lidocaine is 0.1 mg/kg
questions.explanation: For refractory VF/pulseless VT, consider amiodarone; nifekalant or lidocaine may be used as alternatives.

questions.question: Regarding advanced airway management during CPR, select two incorrect statements.
choices: If BVM ventilation is adequate, continuing it is a reasonable option
Endotracheal intubation may be considered when success rates are high
Interruptions to chest compressions during intubation should be kept as brief as possible
Endotracheal intubation is always prioritized over BVM ventilation
After endotracheal intubation, deliver 2 rescue breaths every 30 chest compressions
questions.explanation: Intubation is not always prioritized; after intubation, chest compressions and ventilation are performed asynchronously.

questions.question: After successful endotracheal intubation in an adult cardiac arrest patient, select all correct statements regarding ventilation and chest compressions.
choices: Continue chest compressions at 100–120 per minute
Target approximately 10 breaths per minute for ventilation
Perform chest compressions and ventilation asynchronously
Hyperventilation may reduce cardiac output by increasing intrathoracic pressure
Continue 30:2 ratio even after endotracheal intubation
questions.explanation: After advanced airway placement, continue chest compressions with asynchronous ventilation and avoid hyperventilation.

questions.question: Immediately after endotracheal intubation during CPR, select two correct statements regarding tube position confirmation.
choices: Ideally use waveform capnography in addition to physical examination findings
Waveform capnography cannot be used during resuscitation because exhaled CO₂ is minimal
It is preferable to combine multiple confirmation methods, such as ultrasound confirmation of endotracheal placement
Waveform capnography alone can also rule out endobronchial intubation
Auscultation alone is sufficient during CPR
questions.explanation: It is preferable to use waveform capnography in addition to auscultation and ultrasound.

questions.question: An adult cardiac arrest patient achieves ROSC. Select three incorrect statements regarding post-ROSC management.
choices: Hypoxia should be avoided
Use 100% oxygen for 72 hours after resuscitation
Adjust ventilation to target PaCO₂ within the physiological normal range
Coma is a contraindication to emergent CAG/PCI
Administer antiepileptic drugs prophylactically to all patients to prevent seizures
questions.explanation: Excessive oxygen administration worsens outcomes; oxygen concentration should be adjusted appropriately once SpO₂ and PaO₂ can be confirmed. Coma is not a contraindication to PCI, and prophylactic antiepileptic drug administration is not recommended.

questions.question: Regarding ECPR, select the single most appropriate statement.
choices: It is performed routinely for all adult cardiac arrest patients
Assess ECPR indications before starting BLS
It may be considered for cases meeting certain criteria, such as witnessed ventricular fibrillation, where conventional CPR has not been successful
ECPR is always indicated for asystole
It is a treatment for post-ROSC hypoxia management
questions.explanation: ECPR is not performed for all patients; it is considered for cases that meet eligibility criteria.

questions.question: Regarding targeted temperature management (TTM) after ROSC, select two correct statements.
choices: TTM should be performed in comatose adults after ROSC following out-of-hospital VF cardiac arrest
The target temperature is uniformly 28°C
The target temperature is set within the range of 32–36°C
TTM is completed within 6 hours
Fever after completing TTM is unrelated to prognosis
questions.explanation: TTM should be maintained at 32–36°C for at least 24 hours, and it is recommended to avoid fever even after completing TTM.
